ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general biryani class (FOODON_00002700); no Hyderabadi mutton-specific subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers biryani as a rice-based mixed dish with meat; no Hyderabadi mutton-specific code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Hyderabadi Mutton Dum Biryani or biryani as a food item at this specificity.

preparation_method: dum cooking (slow steam-cooking in sealed pot)
main_ingredients: whole spices (bay leaf, cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, star anise)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Hyderabadi Mutton Dum Biryani is approximately 400 g, comprising a generous mound of fragrant basmati rice layered with bone-in mutton pieces, fried onions, and aromatic spices, typically served with raita and salan.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton Biryani — Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (ICMR-NIN, 2017 revised edition)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) reports values for Mutton Biryani as a composite dish. Hyderabadi Dum Biryani is a regional variant of mutton biryani; values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN mutton biryani entry. Fat content is slightly higher in the Hyderabadi preparation due to ghee and fried onions; values adjusted proportionally within the ICMR-NIN reported range for mutton biryani variants. Serving size of 400 g is based on standard restaurant portion norms for Indian biryani in Singapore.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for basmati rice-based mixed dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Hyderabadi Mutton Dum Biryani. GI estimated at 58 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for basmati rice (GI ~58, medium) as the dominant glycemic carbohydrate source. The protein (mutton) and fat (ghee, fried onions) components lower the overall glycemic response relative to plain rice; however, the basmati rice GI of 58 is used as the primary anchor per standard analogous-food estimation methodology. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (58 × 82.0) / 100 = 47.6 ≈ 47.8 (carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 20.5 × 4 = 82.0 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~1240 mg per restaurant serving of 400 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 200 g per meal, as a full restaurant serving provides approximately 1240 mg sodium — over half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg — and should request reduced salt and no added salan if possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 58) and high glycemic load per standard serving (~47.8 GL units)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Hyderabadi Mutton Dum Biryani in controlled portions (150–200 g) to limit glycemic load, pair it with a low-GI side such as raita or salad, and monitor postprandial blood glucose, as the basmati rice base contributes a medium GI and a high glycemic load at restaurant serving sizes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ghee and mutton (~3.1 g per 100 g; ~12.4 g per 400 g serving) and elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional small portions (150–200 g), as a full restaurant serving provides approximately 12.4 g of saturated fat — approaching the daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association — and should choose lean mutton cuts and request reduced ghee preparation where possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hyderabadi Mutton Dum Biryani is a celebrated slow-cooked rice dish originating from the royal kitchens of Hyderabad, India, and is widely regarded as one of the most iconic biryanis in South Asian cuisine. It is prepared using the 'dum' technique, in which marinated bone-in mutton pieces and parboiled basmati rice are layered in a heavy-bottomed pot or handi, sealed with dough or a tight-fitting lid, and cooked over a low flame so that the steam circulates within the vessel, simultaneously cooking the meat and infusing the rice with the aromas of saffron, whole spices (cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, star anise, bay leaf), fried onions (birista), mint, coriander, and ghee. The traditional Hyderabadi preparation uses the 'kacchi' method, in which raw marinated mutton is layered directly with parboiled rice, distinguishing it from the 'pakki' method used in other regional biryanis. The result is a dish with deeply fragrant, long-grain basmati rice that is fluffy and non-sticky, interspersed with tender, fall-off-the-bone mutton pieces that carry a complex spice profile. The flavour is rich, aromatic, and mildly spiced with a characteristic saffron-and-rose-water finish. Nutritionally, a 400 g restaurant serving provides approximately 740 kcal, 36.8 g protein, 31.2 g fat (including ~12.4 g saturated fat from ghee and mutton), and 82 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is elevated (~1240 mg per serving), making portion control important for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The medium glycemic index (GI ~58) and high glycemic load per serving require careful portion management for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. In Singapore, Hyderabadi Mutton Dum Biryani is served at Indian Muslim and South Indian restaurants and is a popular choice for festive and communal dining.
common_user_queries: What is Hyderabadi Mutton Dum Biryani?
How many calories in mutton dum biryani?
Is mutton biryani good for diabetics?
What is the difference between Hyderabadi biryani and other biryanis?
How much sodium is in mutton biryani?
What spices are used in Hyderabadi biryani?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) entry for Mutton Biryani, which is the closest approved primary source for this dish. No exact ICMR-NIN entry for Hyderabadi Dum Biryani exists; values are adjusted within the reported range for mutton biryani variants to account for the higher ghee and fried onion content characteristic of the Hyderabadi preparation. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database basmati rice entry as the dominant glycemic carbohydrate source. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017), Mutton Biryani entry. No exact Hyderabadi Dum Biryani entry exists in ICMR-NIN; values are adjusted upward for fat and energy to reflect the higher ghee and fried onion content of the Hyderabadi preparation. 2. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database basmati rice entry (GI ~58, medium); no direct GI measurement for Hyderabadi Mutton Dum Biryani was found in approved sources. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. 6. The input food_name 'Hydrabadi Mutton Dum Biryani' contains a common spelling variant; the canonical English name 'Hyderabadi Mutton Dum Biryani' is used in this record, with the input spelling preserved as an alias. 7. Halal status is assumed based on the dish being a traditional Indian Muslim preparation using mutton; individual restaurant halal certification should be verified independently. 8. Serving size of 400 g is based on standard restaurant portion norms for Indian biryani in Singapore; actual restaurant portions may vary.
